segments.title: A personal visit to Enkoji Temple
segments.summary: Clarke leaves his tour group to find Enkoji Temple, where Mitsumasa Yonai is remembered, and explains why the visit matters to him.
segments.body: Clarke travels alone after an earlier attempt with the group failed to locate the destination. He describes the trip as a personal pilgrimage during a wider naval-history tour of Japan. The destination is Enkoji Temple, a modest temple in the city he identifies as the place where Mitsumasa Yonai is remembered. This opening gives the visit its personal purpose before moving to Yonai’s place in the history of the Japanese Navy and the Second World War.

evidence.note: Clarke describes travelling alone to Enkoji Temple to see the place where Yonai is remembered.

segments.title: Yonai as an overlooked Japanese leader
segments.summary: Clarke compares Yonai with other easily overlooked senior naval figures and argues that he deserves more attention in accounts of the Second World War.
segments.body: Clarke compares Yonai with Admiral Henderson, whom he presents as another senior naval figure eclipsed by louder personalities. He argues that Yonai has an important place in Japan’s story but receives less attention in Western recollections of the Second World War. The difficulty of reaching the memorial becomes part of the point: remembering less familiar historical figures can require an intentional journey. Clarke sets this search alongside the tour’s better-known stops associated with Pearl Harbor, the Japanese Navy, and Yamato, explaining why he wants Yonai included in that wider historical picture.

evidence.note: Clarke explains his interest in Yonai, compares him with Admiral Henderson, and contrasts him with more familiar Second World War subjects.
